summary: Evaluate a security education game (incl. safe-AI-coding) vs the Build Week judges and landscape

findings.summary: The security-education-game space is crowded but stratified, and the specific niche the contestant is eyeing — an LLM-native game teaching both classic security AND safe AI-coding practice — is genuinely underserved. Incumbents fall into three buckets: (1) hands-on hacking labs/wargames (TryHackMe, Hack The Box, PortSwigger Web Security Academy, OWASP Juice Shop, picoCTF, OverTheWire), (2) CTF infrastructure (CTFd) and blue-team ranges (Security Blue Team/BTL1, OpenSOC), and (3) enterprise gamified awareness/secure-coding (Secure Code Warrior + 150+ awareness vendors). Their shared, structural weakness is STATIC, HAND-AUTHORED CONTENT: challenges are pre-built, walkthroughs are fixed, once you've solved a room its replay value collapses, and content ages (user-generated THM rooms vary wildly, labs are simplified for pedagogy). An LLM-native design (GPT-5.6 generating fresh scenarios + live adversaries, per-learner adaptive difficulty, Socratic natural-language tutoring, and auto-grading of free-form answers/exploits) attacks exactly that weakness — it turns a fixed problem set into an infinite, personalized one and collapses the two-platform "learn vs. practice" split into one loop. Crucially, the "safe AI usage in coding" topic (prompt injection is OWASP's #1 LLM risk for 2025-2026; CVE-2025-53773 in Copilot, Jan-2026 prompt-injection bugs in external-vendor's own Git MCP server) is almost entirely UN-gamified today: the only sticky consumer artifact is Lakera's Gandalf (a single 8-level prompt-injection game), and the AI-red-team CTFs that exist (HackerOne x HTB "ai_gon3_rogu3", Sep 2025, 217 players; UK AISI challenge) were one-off static events, not adaptive products. The honest risks: gamified security training is a saturated buzzword, LLM auto-generation of security content has a correctness/hallucination problem (a game that teaches a wrong exploit is worse than none), and hosting live vulnerable environments is expensive/hard for a solo builder in a 9-day window.
key_facts: INCUMBENT MAP — Hacking labs/wargames: TryHackMe (skill ACQUISITION; guided walkthroughs then challenges; badges/streaks/leaderboards; 35+ paths; ~$16.99/mo, $126/yr; 2,400+ job placements in 2025) vs Hack The Box (skill VALIDATION; drop-you-on-a-box, figure-it-out; VIP+ only since Oct 2025 at $25/mo). Sources: hackerdna.com/blog/tryhackme-vs-hackthebox, tryhackme.com/compare/hackthebox

PortSwigger Web Security Academy — FREE, browser-based interactive labs covering the full modern AppSec curriculum (SQLi, all XSS, CSRF, business-logic, auth, OAuth, SSRF, race conditions, GraphQL, cache poisoning, request smuggling); text+video theory then exploit a deliberately vulnerable app; works with free Burp Community. The gold standard for structured free web-security teaching. Source: portswigger.net/web-security

OWASP Juice Shop — free open-source deliberately-vulnerable Node/Angular app; covers OWASP Top 10 + more; gamified Score Board with achievement-style challenge unlocks; optional CTF flag mode; built-in 'Hacking Instructor' tutorial scripts; 33 challenges have a 'Find It' coding-challenge phase teaching you to spot the bug in the actual source. Self-hosted. Sources: owasp.org/www-project-juice-shop, juice-shop.github.io

picoCTF (Carnegie Mellon) — free, aimed at high-school/college beginners, non-competitive, tutorials, reversing/crypto/hacking puzzles. OverTheWire (Bandit etc.) — free SSH-based wargames teaching Linux CLI, networking, basic exploitation via progressively harder levels. Common beginner ladder: OverTheWire Bandit -> picoCTF -> TryHackMe -> HTB. Sources: overthewire.org/wargames, hackerdna.com/blog/overthewire-wargames-guide

CTFd — dominant open-source CTF FRAMEWORK (not curriculum): custom challenges/categories/hints/flags, dynamic scoring, unlockables, plugins/themes, individual+team, scoreboards, bruteforce protection; managed hosting available. It's the plumbing others build on. Blue-team side: OpenSOC (free network-defense simulation), Security Blue Team's BTL1 (defensive analyst cert/training). Sources: ctfd.io, github.com/ctfd/ctfd, opensoc.io

Secure Code Warrior — the closest incumbent to the contestant's DEV-SECURITY angle: gamified enterprise secure-coding platform; Tournaments with time limits, lives, live leaderboards, prizes; 'Devlympics' global free tournament; challenges based on real code, find/locate/fix vulns. But it's static hand-authored content and enterprise-B2B, not a public game and not AI-coding-safety focused. Source: securecodewarrior.com/products/tournaments

SHARED WEAKNESS of incumbents = static, hand-authored, aging content: THM rooms 'sometimes feel rushed or outdated,' user-generated quality varies; labs 'simplified for educational purposes' don't fully prep for real-world (skill-decay risk); HTB forces you across TWO platforms (Academy to learn, Main App to practice). Replay value collapses once a challenge is solved. Sources: hackerdna.com/blog/tryhackme-vs-hackthebox, fusioncyber.co blog

AI-CODING-SECURITY is the un-gamified frontier: prompt injection is OWASP's #1 LLM risk 2025/2026; 73% of assessed AI systems exposed; GitHub Copilot RCE via prompt injection (CVE-2025-53773); Jan-2026 prompt-injection bugs found in external-vendor's OWN official Git MCP server; 83% of orgs plan agentic AI but only 29% feel ready to do it securely. Real, current, high-stakes teaching material. Sources: securance.com/blog/prompt-injection-the-owasp-1-ai-threat-in-2026, arxiv.org/html/2601.17548v1, securecodewarrior.com/article/prompt-injection-and-the-security-risks-of-agentic-coding-tools

The ONLY sticky AI-security GAME today is Lakera's Gandalf (gandalf.lakera.ai) — a single-mechanic, 8-level prompt-injection game (extract a password past escalating guardrails). Massively popular but narrow, static, and vendor-marketing. AI-red-team CTFs that exist were ONE-OFF static events: HackerOne x Hack The Box 'ai_gon3_rogu3' (Sep 2025, 504 reg / 217 active, 11 jeopardy challenges mapped to OWASP LLM Top 10; completion dropped 98%->34% across difficulty; bimodal skill split ~1/3 solved everything); UK AISI + OpenAI/external-vendor/DeepMind AI Agent Red Teaming Challenge (Mar-Apr 2025). None are adaptive products. Sources: hackthebox.com/blog/ai-red-teaming-ctf-competition, gandalf.lakera.ai

MARKET SIZE / SATURATION: Security awareness training market ~$6.74B (2026), 16.82% CAGR to ~$14.66B by 2031; the GAMIFIED sub-segment is small — ~$430M (2025) -> ~$675M (2030) at only 8.8% CAGR. 150+ active awareness providers globally = 'crowded.' Broader AI security market projected $122.6B by 2033. 'Gamified security training' is a saturated marketing phrase; the AI-NATIVE slice is nascent. Sources: mordorintelligence.com/industry-reports/security-awareness-training-market, adaptivesecurity.com blog

LLM-NATIVE tutoring/generation is still RESEARCH-STAGE, not a shipped consumer product: papers on GenAI intelligent tutoring for cybersecurity (SENSAI, GPT-4o Socratic mentor), AgentCyTE (agentic generation of executable threat environments, 'months of expert effort to days'), AEGIS (LLM attack-path generation for cyber-defense exercises), plus 2026 in-situ studies of students using AI tutors in CTFs. This confirms the capability is real and validated academically but NO incumbent owns the adaptive-generation + tutoring + auto-grading loop as a product. Sources: arxiv.org/pdf/2510.25189, arxiv.org/pdf/2405.04138, arxiv.org/pdf/2602.17448
whats_missing: A gamified, HANDS-ON curriculum for USING AI CODING AGENTS SAFELY (prompt injection via poisoned repos/issues/MCP tools, over-permissioned agents, insecure-output-handling, secret exfiltration, malicious 'skills'/MCP servers). This is OWASP's #1 LLM risk and is exploding in real CVEs, yet the only game is Gandalf's single password-extraction mechanic. A game where the learner plays BOTH the attacker poisoning an agent's context AND the defender configuring guardrails/permissions is essentially open territory — and it maps directly to the contestant's own MCP-server-building expertise.

INFINITE / NON-MEMORIZABLE content. Every incumbent's challenges are static and get spoiled by writeups within days (THM/HTB/Juice Shop solutions are all on GitHub/Medium). GPT-5.6 generating a fresh scenario, adversary persona, and vulnerable artifact per session — so no two playthroughs and no writeup can be looked up — is a capability none of them structurally have.

A LIVE, REASONING ADVERSARY. Gandalf's defense is a fixed guardrail; HTB boxes don't fight back. An LLM opponent that adapts its social-engineering / evasion / defense to the learner's specific moves (and can role-play a phishing attacker, a manipulative code-review bot, a hardening blue-teamer) is a mechanic that only an LLM enables.

UNIFIED learn->practice->assess loop with natural-language tutoring. Incumbents split guided teaching (walkthroughs) from unguided challenge, and grade only by static flag-matching. Free-form auto-grading ('explain WHY this is vulnerable and write the fix / the exploit', graded on reasoning not a flag string) plus Socratic hinting that adapts to the learner's misconception is missing at the product level — it exists only in research prototypes.

PERSONALIZED adaptive difficulty that responds to the bimodal skill gap the HTB CTF exposed (a third solve everything, many solve almost nothing). A generator that dials scenario complexity to the individual — instead of one fixed difficulty curve everyone shares — directly addresses a documented failure of current CTFs.

A bridge for the huge population of NON-security 'vibe coders' now shipping AI-generated code. Existing platforms target aspiring security professionals; there is little that meets a general/junior developer where they are and teaches them not to get owned by their own AI tooling. That audience is far larger than the CTF crowd and currently unserved by any game.
implications: Pick the AI-CODING-SAFETY lane, not generic 'gamified security.' Competing head-on with TryHackMe/HTB/PortSwigger on classic hacking labs is a saturated, content-heavy losing battle for a solo builder in 9 days. But 'learn to use AI coding agents without getting owned' is a fresh, high-urgency niche (OWASP #1, live CVEs, only Gandalf competing) that plays directly to the contestant's red+blue + MCP-server background and to the hackathon's own tech (Codex/GPT-5.6, agentic coding). Strong fit for Developer Tools AND Education tracks simultaneously.

The winning wedge is DYNAMIC GENERATION + LIVE ADVERSARY + AUTO-GRADING — the three things static incumbents cannot retrofit. Frame the demo around 'no two playthroughs are the same and you cannot Google the answer,' which is instantly legible to judges and structurally impossible for THM/HTB/Juice Shop.

Correctness is the make-or-break risk and must be designed for, not hand-waved. A game that teaches a hallucinated or wrong exploit/fix is a negative. Constrain generation with a curated ground-truth spine (OWASP LLM Top 10 categories, real CVE patterns) and have the model generate VARIATIONS on verified templates rather than free-inventing vulns; consider a verifier/self-check pass. This is also a compelling thing to show judges as evidence of security seriousness.

Scope for the 9-day build by AVOIDING live vulnerable infrastructure. Gandalf-style (chat against an LLM you must break) and 'spot/fix the vuln in generated code' loops need no VMs, no container ranges, no cloud lab cost — they run entirely on model calls, which is exactly what the contestant has access to and de-risks the timeline versus HTB-style hosted boxes.

Sticky mechanics to borrow from incumbents that translate to LLM-native: Score Board/achievement unlocks (Juice Shop), tournaments with lives + live leaderboard (Secure Code Warrior), streaks/badges (THM), and the play-as-both-red-and-blue framing (Gandalf's original blue-team/red-team split). Combine these with per-learner generation for retention no static platform can match.

Position against the market honestly: 'gamified security awareness' is only an ~8.8% CAGR / sub-$1B backwater within a big market, and it's crowded with 150+ awareness vendors. Do NOT pitch as awareness training. Pitch as an AI-native developer-security skill game — the intersection that is empirically empty (research prototypes only, no shipped product) — which is both a cleaner story and a more defensible claim to judges.
cliches_to_avoid: 'Gamified cybersecurity training' as a headline — it's a saturated buzzword with 150+ vendors; leading with it makes the project sound generic and me-too.
'Duolingo for hacking / for security' — this analogy is already overused by multiple existing platforms and pitches; it signals derivative thinking to judges.

Claiming to 'replace' TryHackMe/Hack The Box/PortSwigger — they own hands-on hacking-skill acquisition; a solo 9-day build cannot and the claim invites unflattering comparison. Position as complementary and in a different lane.
Positioning as enterprise 'security awareness training' (phishing-simulation style) — low-growth segment, brutally crowded (KnowBe4 et al.), and it undersells the genuinely novel AI-native angle.

'AI-powered' as a vague garnish. Every vendor now claims AI. The differentiation must be concrete and demonstrable (generation you can't Google, adversary that adapts to your move, grading of reasoning not flags) or it reads as buzzword bingo.

Over-promising realism ('train real-world red-teamers'). Simplified generated scenarios are a documented limitation of even the big platforms; claiming production-grade realism from a hackathon build is dishonest and easily punctured.

findings.summary: The "safe AI usage in coding" space is HOT but LOPSIDED. There is massive 2026 activity on the THREAT side (OpenClaw/ClawHub crisis, slopsquatting, MCP poisoning, secret sprawl) and a growing wall of PASSIVE education (Udemy courses, checklists, blog guides, the Practical DevSecOps CMCPSE cert). The GAMIFIED/interactive corner is real but narrow: nearly every existing game teaches ONE skill — writing a prompt-injection payload to extract a secret (Lakera Gandalf, CrowdStrike "AI Unlocked"/SAIGE, Agent 0DIN, Damn Vulnerable LLM Agent). These are RED-TEAM games: they teach you to ATTACK a chatbot. Almost NOBODY has a game that teaches the everyday developer the DEFENDER/operator workflow — "here is your coding agent's proposed action / this MCP skill / this suggested dependency: do you approve, sandbox, or reject, and why?" That defender-side, workflow-hygiene angle (approval fatigue, over-broad permissions, unsandboxed exec, trusting ClawHub-style skills, installing hallucinated packages) is where a game would be genuinely novel. Topicality to the OpenAI Codex team is about as high as it gets: OpenAI published "Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection," published "Running Codex safely" (May 2026), ships Codex with explicit sandbox + approval "two dials," Codex itself had disclosed CVEs (BeyondTrust GitHub-token theft; CVE-2025-59536; CVE-2026-21852), and the OpenAI Developer Community already hosts Codex red-team skills. A game literally titled "don't get owned by your AI agent" maps 1:1 onto OpenAI's own stated safety framing.
key_facts: OpenAI has publicly staked out this exact topic: it published 'Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection' (openai.com/index/designing-agents-to-resist-prompt-injection/) and 'Running Codex safely at OpenAI' (~May 8 2026), and ships Codex with two explicit safety 'dials' — a sandbox (what Codex CAN do) and an approval policy (WHEN it must stop and ask). Sources: contextstudios.ai/blog/running-codex-safely-openai-security-playbook, anomity.ai/blog/securing-openai-codex-sandbox-and-approvals-guide, azukiazusa.dev/en/blog/codex-sandbox-agent-authorization

Codex itself has real, disclosed vulnerabilities — this is not theoretical for the Codex team. BeyondTrust Phantom Labs disclosed a command-injection in Codex that stole GitHub User Access Tokens via a malicious branch name; Check Point Research (Feb 25 2026) documented CVE-2025-59536 (project code executed before trust-dialog approval, CVSS 8.7) and CVE-2026-21852 (attacker-controlled OPENAI_BASE_URL leaked API keys pre-trust). Sources: cybedefend.com/en/blog/openai-codex-security-risks-best-practices, github.com/mturac/everything-openai-codex/blob/main/the-security-guide.md

OpenClaw/ClawHub is the confirmed cultural watershed. Palo Alto Unit 42 (unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/openclaw-ai-supply-chain-risk/) and The Hacker News (thehackernews.com/2026/02/researchers-find-341-malicious-clawhub.html) document 341+ malicious ClawHub skills (Koi Security), ~12-20% of the registry, delivering Atomic macOS Stealer (AMOS). CVE-2026-25253 (CVSS 8.8, RCE, patched in OpenClaw 2026.1.29 on 30 Jan 2026); 30,000-40,000+ internet-exposed / no-auth instances (Censys, Bitsight, Hunt.io); Snyk reportedly found ~1,467 malicious skills (91% combining prompt injection with traditional malware); Cisco found 9 vulns in the #1 most-downloaded skill. Unit 42 explicitly frames this as an AI supply-chain 'paradigm shift' with novel 'agentic threats.'

The gamified education that EXISTS is almost all attacker-side prompt-injection extraction games, not defender-workflow games: Lakera Gandalf (gandalf.lakera.ai, 1M+ players, 8 levels, extract a hidden password); CrowdStrike 'AI Unlocked: Decoding Prompt Injection' vs the SAIGE chatbot (crowdstrike.com, launched ~July 2026, 3 environments, score by tokens used); Agent 0DIN gamified CTF (0din.ai/blog/agent-0din); OWASP FinBot CTF; Damn Vulnerable LLM Agent (ReAct Thought/Action/Observation injection). All teach 'craft a payload to break a bot' — none simulate the developer's own approve/deny/sandbox decisions.

OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) is the ready-made curriculum spine: LLM01 Prompt Injection (#1), LLM05 Insecure Output Handling, plus 2025 additions Excessive Agency, System Prompt Leakage, Vector/Embedding Weaknesses, Misinformation, Unbounded Consumption. There is also a nascent OWASP MCP Top 10 (tool poisoning, OAuth abuse, supply chain) referenced by Practical DevSecOps. Sources: owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications, practical-devsecops.com/mcp-security-vulnerabilities

The developer-workflow harms are quantified and vivid — great game mechanics. Slopsquatting: 20% of 576k AI-generated samples referenced non-existent packages (205,474 unique hallucinated names; 43% recur across 10 queries), with real attacks like ccxt-mexc-futures hiding credential stealers (venturebeat.com, trendmicro.com). Secret sprawl: GitGuardian 'State of Secrets Sprawl 2026' — 29M secrets leaked on public GitHub in 2025 (+34% YoY); AI-assisted commits leak secrets at 3.2% vs 1.5% human-only; Apiiro found AI-assisted devs leaked Azure keys at ~2x the rate. AI-code vuln rate ~24.7-45% (Cloud Security Alliance). Sources: gitguardian via kusari.dev, labs.cloudsecurityalliance.org

'Approval fatigue' is a documented, named failure mode and an ideal core game loop. Research shows human-in-the-loop approval defenses fail because developers rubber-stamp dozens of prompts per session ('Lies-in-the-Loop', Checkmarx; arxiv 2605.17634 'AI Agents May Always Fall for Prompt Injections'). Agentic editors (Cursor auto-run, VSCode 'Auto Approve' with empty deny-list) auto-execute — a game that trains the reflex to actually READ the diff/command before approving fills a real behavioral gap.

Formal/passive training is crowding in fast (saturation warning for non-game formats): Practical DevSecOps 'Certified MCP Security Expert (CMCPSE)' — claims first dedicated hands-on MCP cert (60 days labs, 30+ exercises, in-browser live MCP targets); multiple 2026 Udemy courses ('Prompt Injection & LLM Defense 2026', 'The Vibe Coding Security and Risk Course'); Class Central lists 200+ prompt-injection courses; Hacksplaining has an 'AI: Prompt Injection' secure-coding lesson; appsecco/vulnerable-mcp-servers-lab and ReversingLabs vulnerable-MCP lab on GitHub. Interactive DEFENDER-workflow gaming is the un-crowded slice.
whats_missing: A DEFENDER/operator game, not an attacker game. Everything shipping (Gandalf, SAIGE, Agent 0DIN, DVLA) puts the player in the attacker seat crafting injection payloads. Nobody gamifies the developer's real daily decisions: 'Your agent wants to run `curl … | bash` — approve/sandbox/deny?', 'This ClawHub-style skill requests ~/.ssh and .env read — install?', 'Codex suggests `pip install ccxt-mexc-futures` — does this package even exist?'. This defender-reflex training is the open, novel lane.

A game that teaches the SANDBOX + APPROVAL mental model that OpenAI itself ships in Codex. OpenAI's 'two dials' (sandbox = what it CAN do; approval = WHEN it asks) and least-privilege/blast-radius framing is begging for a puzzle/tower-defense/incremental mechanic. No existing game teaches 'engineer the blast radius' as play — yet that is exactly OpenAI's stated defense philosophy ('prompt injection is a system-design problem, not a prompt-design problem').

Slopsquatting / hallucinated-dependency detection as a game mechanic. Huge, quantified, visceral threat (205k+ hallucinated package names) with essentially zero gamified training. A 'real package or AI hallucination?' fast-judgment mode, or a supply-chain 'inspect the setup.py before you install' mode, is wide open and directly teaches a behavior that prevents real compromise.

MCP-server / skill-marketplace trust decisions as play. The OpenClaw/ClawHub crisis is THE 2026 story and maps perfectly to a game ('audit this skill's manifest vs its actual network calls; is the publisher who they claim?'). Unit 42's recommended workflow (verify provenance, line-by-line source audit, watch outbound traffic) is literally a set of game levels no one has built.

'Approval fatigue' simulated as an escalating-pressure mechanic. The best-documented human failure mode (rubber-stamping under time pressure) has no game that recreates the pressure and trains resistance to it. A timed/streak mechanic where a malicious action is smuggled among benign ones would teach the exact reflex the research says humans lack.

An indirect-prompt-injection-via-untrusted-context level set aimed at the coding-agent context specifically (poisoned README/issue/PR/dependency docs/config files that the agent reads and obeys), rather than the generic 'trick the chatbot' framing. This is the Codex-relevant attack surface and is under-gamified.

A cohesive narrative/curriculum that unifies the whole OWASP LLM Top 10 + MCP Top 10 into progressive levels. Existing games each isolate one vulnerability class; a single game that walks a developer from 'leaked secret' → 'over-broad permission' → 'malicious skill' → 'hallucinated dep' → 'unsandboxed RCE' as a connected kill-chain does not appear to exist.
implications: Topicality to the OpenAI Codex team is close to maximal — build with confidence. OpenAI has publicly published on resisting prompt injection AND on running Codex safely, ships the exact sandbox+approval model your game would teach, and has fielded real Codex CVEs. A game that dramatizes 'don't get owned by your AI agent' is on-message with OpenAI's own words, not a stretch. Lean into the Codex sandbox/approval 'two dials' as your teaching model — it flatters and aligns with the judges' own product.

Differentiate hard on DEFENDER-side workflow. Do NOT build another 'trick the chatbot to reveal the password' game — that space is saturated (Gandalf has 1M+ players; CrowdStrike just shipped SAIGE in July 2026; Agent 0DIN, DVLA exist). Your unique wedge is the approve/sandbox/reject decision loop the developer actually faces. Frame it explicitly as the counterpoint to Gandalf: 'Gandalf teaches you to attack; this teaches you not to get owned.'

Anchor the game in the OpenClaw/ClawHub crisis for instant cultural resonance with a July 2026 hackathon audience — but be careful with names. The crisis is real and heavily sourced (Unit 42, Hacker News, Snyk, Cisco, Censys), so use it as the framing 'moment.' Consider a fictionalized-but-obvious marketplace to avoid legal/trademark issues while keeping the recognizability.

Use the quantified harms as built-in scoring/stakes: slopsquatting hit-rates (20% hallucinated packages), secret-leak deltas (AI commits leak 2x+), the 341+ malicious ClawHub skills. Real numbers as level goals/leaderboards give the game credibility with a security-literate judge and let your DFIR/red-team background show.

Make 'approval fatigue' the emotional core. It is the best-documented human failure ('Lies-in-the-Loop', 'AI Agents May Always Fall for Prompt Injections') and the most game-able — escalating time pressure, streaks, a malicious action hidden among benign ones. This is the single mechanic most likely to feel novel AND teach a behavior change, which is what an education-track judge rewards.

Scope realistically for a solo build July 13-21 with a fresh Codex setup: OWASP LLM Top 10 + MCP threats give a ready curriculum, so you don't need to invent taxonomy — pick 4-6 level archetypes (approve/deny command, audit MCP skill manifest, spot hallucinated dep, catch indirect injection in a PR/README, size the sandbox blast radius) and ship those deeply rather than covering all 10. Meta-move worth a line in your submission: build the game USING Codex and narrate the safe-agent practices you followed — dogfooding the lesson is a strong Developer-Tools/Education crossover story.

Honesty caveat to keep the submission credible: 'AI security awareness game' as a phrase is somewhat saturated (200+ courses, several CTFs, multiple vendor games). Your novelty is specific — defender workflow + Codex's own sandbox/approval model + the ClawHub supply-chain moment — so state that precisely rather than claiming to be the first AI-security game, which a knowledgeable judge would immediately discount.
cliches_to_avoid: Another 'jailbreak the chatbot / extract the secret password' prompt-injection game — this is the single most saturated format (Lakera Gandalf alone has 1M+ players, and CrowdStrike shipped SAIGE in July 2026). Doing it again reads as derivative.

Claiming to be 'the first game to teach AI/LLM security' — false and easily disproven by a security-literate judge (Gandalf, Agent 0DIN, DVLA, OWASP FinBot CTF, CrowdStrike AI Unlocked all predate you).

Generic 'AI security awareness' framing with no specific threat model — the space is full of vague courses and checklists; a hackathon judge will pattern-match it to noise unless you name concrete mechanics (approve/deny, MCP manifest audit, slopsquatting detection).
Leaning on 'vibe coding is dangerous' as the whole thesis — it's now a 2026 cliché phrase with dozens of think-pieces and courses; use the concept but don't make the tired term your headline.

A dry quiz/flashcard 'gamification' (points and badges bolted onto a course) — the market already has this (Udemy, Class Central 200+, Hacksplaining). If it isn't a genuine decision-simulation, it won't feel like a game.
Fear-mongering stat-dumping with no playable decision — reciting the 340% / 29M-secrets / 341-skills numbers without turning them into stakes the player acts on is a common, hollow pattern.

findings.summary: A security-learning GAME can win the OpenAI Build Week Education track — but only if it reads as a credible learning PRODUCT with a pedagogy and a measurable outcome, not an arcade with points and badges. The actual hackathon rubric (fetched from openai.devpost.com) is four equal criteria judged by OpenAI/Codex staff: Technological Implementation ("how thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex?"), Design ("a complete, coherent product experience"), Potential Impact ("a credible, specific case for solving a real problem for a real audience"), and Quality of the Idea ("creative... genuine understanding of the problem space"). The strongest concept for this contestant is a game teaching SAFE AI-ASSISTED CODING — how to use coding agents (Codex, etc.) without getting owned by prompt injection — because it sits at the exact intersection of (a) Leah Belsky's stated doctrine that "every graduate needs to know how to use AI" and specifically that vibe coding requires "technical verification knowledge," (b) the #1 OWASP LLM threat of 2026 (prompt injection, a real audience with real losses), and (c) the contestant's genuine red/blue-team expertise. To beat the "toy risk," the game must use GPT-5.6 as a live Socratic adversary/tutor (adaptive scenarios, scaffolded hints, feedback that explains WHY a mistake was dangerous, and a mandatory reflection step) and must ship with a pre/post measurement of learning — that combination is what converts "game" from gimmick to credible ed-product in front of a technically elite panel, and it simultaneously maxes the "thoroughly uses Codex/GPT-5.6" criterion that a static CTF cannot claim.
key_facts: ACTUAL RUBRIC (openai.devpost.com, fetched 2026-07-13): four criteria, judged by OpenAI/Codex staff. (1) Technological Implementation: 'How thoroughly and skillfully does the project use Codex?' (2) Design: 'Does the project deliver a working or runnable project that has a complete, coherent product experience?' (3) Potential Impact: 'Does the project make a credible, specific case for solving a real problem for a real audience?' (4) Quality of the Idea: 'Is the project creative? Does the team demonstrate genuine understanding of the problem space?' Must use Codex + GPT-5.6; demo video <3 min on YouTube must explain BOTH; must submit a /feedback Codex Session ID; deadline July 21 2026 5pm PDT; 1st per track $15k, 2nd $10k.

BELSKY/OPENAI DOCTRINE that a winning ed project should echo: Study Mode's stated pedagogy is active participation, managing cognitive load, proactively developing metacognition + self-reflection, fostering curiosity, and actionable/supportive feedback — via the Socratic method (questions and hints, NOT answers) with scaffolding calibrated to the learner's stage, personalized to prior chats/objectives/skill (openai.com Study Mode page; corroborated by mlq.ai and infoq.com).

BELSKY'S SIGNATURE LINE, directly quotable in the pitch: 'if students use AI as an answer machine, they are not going to learn'; AI should 'expand critical thinking and expand creativity'; 'every graduate... needs to know how to use AI.' She EXPLICITLY endorses teaching vibe coding AND says it 'requires some technical verification knowledge' (tekedia.com). That last clause is the exact thesis of a safe-AI-coding game.

BELSKY'S BROADER PRIORITIES (map your 'Potential Impact' framing to these): access/equity and democratizing opportunity; certifications (AI Foundations for workers, a ChatGPT course for K-12 teachers) delivered inside ChatGPT and via free OpenAI Academy; the OpenAI Jobs Platform (mid-2026); the goal of '10 million Americans certified by 2030'; connecting 'learning, certification, and real economic opportunity in one clear journey' (LinkedIn/ETIH EdTech News; openai.com 'Expanding economic opportunity with AI').

PRIOR OPENAI EDUCATION SHOWCASE (NextGenAI, $50M): funded REAL-PROBLEM tools — rare-disease diagnosis time reduction (Harvard/Boston Children's), library text digitization (Oxford Bodleian, Boston Public Library), AI literacy initiatives (Texas A&M) — not games or toys. The showcase bar rewards measurable, real-world-anchored impact (openai.com/index/introducing-nextgenai).

SECURITY-GAME LEARNING EVIDENCE: CTF/gamification research shows gains in knowledge, self-efficacy, interest, engagement and confidence — BUT the Finland/Czechia study and others find learners rate the CTF TASKS and instructions higher than the gamification elements (points/badges) for meaningful learning. Substance beats arcade mechanics (link.springer.com; Wiley cae.70082; doi.org/10.3390/jcp6010016).

DOCUMENTED FAILURE MODES of security games (arxiv.org/pdf/2004.11556) — the exact things elite judges will smell as gimmick: solving by trial-and-error guessing without understanding; unscaffolded/uneven difficulty (trivial OR impossible); no structured feedback; no reflection step; experienced players dominate and novices bounce. Fixes: progressive scaffolding + hints, feedback that explains WHY a solution failed, and a MANDATORY post-challenge reflection/writeup.

SERIOUS-GAMES MEASUREMENT LITERATURE: credibility comes from EVALUATION. Combine completion assessment + in-process assessment (choices during play) + pre/post learning measures; authentic task environments elicit real ability. Weak evidence = indirect measures without psychometric grounding — avoid claiming 'it teaches' with no data (link.springer.com s11423-024-10362-0; journal.seriousgamessociety.org).

SUBJECT-MATTER CURRENCY (why the topic is a real problem for a real audience in 2026): prompt injection is the OWASP #1 LLM threat of 2026 and is treated as 'an unsolved problem rather than a bug awaiting a patch'; prompt-injection attacks up ~340% YoY; coding agents (external-agent Code, Gemini CLI, Codex, Cline, Aider) drive most new attack data; a malicious GitHub repo can own a developer machine with ZERO malicious code via indirect prompt injection; 35% of real incidents came from simple prompts, some causing >$100k damage (helpnetsecurity.com 2026-06-29 & 2026-06-11; securance.com; cycode.com).

LLM-NATIVE GAME DESIGN is the differentiator: GPT is used in >85% of educational-application studies; LLM-driven NPCs, adaptive scaffolding, and personalized error-specific feedback are the frontier — but research warns LLMs still struggle to simulate imperfect, learner-specific reasoning, so design the AI as an adaptive Socratic ADVERSARY/tutor rather than an omniscient oracle (cescg.org LLM-driven NPCs; arxiv 2509.15068; arxiv 2507.02180).

CREDIBLE REFERENCE MODELS to cite as prior art you improve on: PortSwigger Web Security Academy (expert-authored by the Web App Hacker's Handbook author, structured learning paths, interactive labs — credible, NOT gimmicky) and Secure Code Warrior (gamified secure-coding training at scale). Position your project as 'these, but LLM-native and about AI-agent safety' (portswigger.net/web-security; securecodewarrior.com).
whats_missing: A learning game specifically about SAFE AI-ASSISTED CODING / using coding agents without getting owned. The security-training market (PortSwigger, Secure Code Warrior, HTB, TryHackMe) covers classic web/appsec and CTFs; almost nothing gamifies the 2026-current threat of indirect prompt injection against coding agents — the exact topic Belsky flagged (vibe coding needs 'verification knowledge') and OWASP's #1 LLM risk. This is a genuinely open, defensible niche.

An LLM-native security game where GPT-5.6 plays an adaptive ADVERSARY that injects prompts, plants poisoned repos/docs, and social-engineers the learner in real time — most existing CTFs are static, pre-authored challenges. A live generative adversary is both novel and a direct, honest use of GPT-5.6/Codex (maxes the Technological Implementation criterion).

A security learning tool with a BUILT-IN measurable outcome (pre/post skill delta, a mastery certificate, or a shareable 'I survived the injection gauntlet' score tied to specific competencies). Most hackathon games ship engagement, not evidence — shipping even a small pre/post assessment separates you from the toy pile and speaks Belsky's 'measurable/certification' language.

A defender-side (blue-team) angle: games overwhelmingly teach offense (exploit the flag). A game that teaches you to REVIEW AI-generated code for planted vulnerabilities, spot a prompt-injected diff, or configure agent guardrails is under-served and plays directly to this contestant's blue-team credibility.

Equity/access framing for a security game: free, browser-runnable, no-install, works for a non-security-expert developer (not just CTF veterans). The 'novices bounce' failure mode is an opening — a game that onboards a normal developer to AI-agent safety serves Belsky's access/equity priority in a way hardcore CTFs do not.
implications: DO frame it as a learning PRODUCT, not a game, in the pitch: lead with the pedagogy (Socratic, scaffolded, reflective) and the measurable outcome, then reveal it's fun. Judges scoring 'Potential Impact' and 'Design' reward a coherent product experience with a credible audience; they discount arcades. Say 'security training tool that happens to be a game,' never 'a fun security game.'

DO pick SAFE AI-ASSISTED CODING as the topic (prompt injection against coding agents, poisoned repos, malicious MCP/tool responses, reviewing AI-written code). It is the single strongest choice: it is OWASP #1 in 2026 (real problem, real audience with real $ losses), it is literally what Belsky said graduates must learn, it is Codex/agent-native, and it is where THIS contestant's red+blue expertise is unfakeable.

DO make GPT-5.6 the adaptive Socratic ADVERSARY and tutor — it generates fresh injection attempts, adapts difficulty to the learner, and after each round explains WHY the attack worked and what defense would have stopped it. This single design choice simultaneously (a) maxes 'thoroughly uses Codex/GPT-5.6,' (b) implements Study Mode's exact pedagogy, and (c) is something a static CTF structurally cannot do — your novelty and your tech story become the same thing.

DO ship an explicit feedback + reflection loop after every challenge (the #1 evidence-backed difference between games that teach and games that don't per arxiv 2004.11556). Never let the learner 'win' by guessing; make the AI ask them to articulate what happened. This is cheap to build and is exactly what separates credible from gimmick to an elite panel.

DO include a real measured outcome even if small: a 5-question pre-test and the same post-test, a competency checklist, and a shareable mastery result. In the demo, SHOW the score delta. This is the highest-leverage, lowest-cost move to score 'Potential Impact' — it turns 'I think it teaches' into 'it measurably teaches.' It also speaks Belsky's certification/measurable-skills language.

DO exploit the 3-minute video ruthlessly: open on the real stakes (a malicious repo owning a dev machine via a coding agent — a real 2026 headline), show ONE complete play-through with the AI adversary adapting, show the WHY-feedback, and end on the pre/post score jump. State plainly how Codex built it and how GPT-5.6 runs the adversary/tutor loop (both are required in the video).

DON'T lead with points, badges, XP bars, or leaderboards as the core loop. Research shows learners value the tasks over the gamification, and technical judges read heavy game-juice as compensating for thin substance. Keep mechanics minimal and in service of the learning.

DON'T build an offense-only exploit-the-flag CTF clone — it's saturated (PortSwigger, HTB, TryHackMe) and reads as derivative under 'Quality of the Idea.' Differentiate with the defender/code-review angle and the LLM-adversary novelty.

DON'T over-scope the game world (levels, story, art). With a solo build July 13-21 and Codex/API access only being set up today, one polished, deep scenario with a working adaptive AI adversary and a measured outcome beats ten shallow static levels. Judges reward a complete coherent slice over a broad broken one (the 'Design' criterion is explicitly about a runnable, coherent experience).

DON'T make it require expert prior knowledge or local install — a novice-friendly, browser-runnable, free tool serves Belsky's access/equity priority and avoids the documented 'novices bounce' failure mode; gate difficulty with scaffolding instead of assuming CTF fluency.

DON'T claim unverified learning gains — a technically elite panel will catch it. If you measure, measure honestly (n even = your own test runs) and present it as a designed evaluation loop, which itself signals seriousness.
cliches_to_avoid: 'Duolingo for cybersecurity' / 'Duolingo for X' — the single most overused ed-tech hackathon framing; signals lack of original thought to a jaded panel.
Generic points/badges/XP/streaks/leaderboard gamification presented as the innovation — saturated and read as gimmick by technical judges.
'Fun, engaging, interactive way to learn security' as the value prop — every ed game says this; it is not a differentiator and dodges the pedagogy and outcome questions.
A me-too CTF / capture-the-flag clone — the space is heavily saturated (PortSwigger, Hack The Box, TryHackMe, picoCTF); offense-only flag hunting is not novel.
'Teaching kids to code safely' generic K-12 framing — dilutes the sharp, defensible, expert-credible AI-coding-agent-security niche into a crowded, less impressive category.
Leaning on a cute mascot / narrative wrapper as the hook — art and story burn scarce solo build time and read as compensating for thin learning substance.
Claiming 'measurable outcomes' in the pitch without actually showing a pre/post number in the demo — an elite panel will notice the gap immediately.

design.direction_verdict: SMART PIVOT — but only in one specific shape. A generic "gamified security training" project is a trap that lands as a toy for Sottiaux ("scaffolding is coping") and Steinberger (slideware-allergic, wants agents that close their own loop). The pivot wins ONLY if the game's ENGINE is itself a verification-layer system: GPT-5.6 generates a fresh scenario, then a separate critic pass grades the player's free-form reasoning/fix against a curated ground-truth spine and produces receipts for why it's right or wrong. Framed that way, the "game" IS the panel's favorite artifact class — an agent that critiques its own output and proves the verdict — wearing a delightful skin. The topicality upside is close to maximal for THIS panel specifically: OpenAI published "Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection" and "Running Codex safely," ships Codex with the sandbox+approval "two dials," and has fielded real Codex CVEs (BeyondTrust token theft, CVE-2025-59536, CVE-2026-21852). A game that dramatizes the exact defender workflow OpenAI ships is on-message with the judges' own product, and it uniquely lets the contestant's real red+blue+MCP moat show. Net verdict: commit, but market it as "a decision-simulation with a verification engine that grades your reasoning against ground truth," never as "a fun security game." Do NOT build another Gandalf-style jailbreak-the-bot game (saturated: 1M+ players, CrowdStrike shipped SAIGE July 2026) — the empty, defensible lane is the DEFENDER/operator seat: approve, sandbox, or reject.

concepts.name: Blast Radius
concepts.pitch: A decision-simulation that drops you in the operator seat of a coding agent and grades your approve / sandbox / reject calls against verified ground truth, so you learn to size the blast radius before your agent gets owned.
concepts.gameplay_loop: Each round GPT-5.6 generates a fresh proposed agent action from a realistic session — a command (curl X | bash), a suggested dependency (pip install <maybe-hallucinated>), an MCP/skill manifest requesting ~/.ssh + .env, a diff that quietly adds a data-exfil call, or a poisoned README the agent is about to obey. Under a mild timer that mimics real approval fatigue, the player picks APPROVE / SANDBOX (and configures the blast radius — which paths, net egress, which capabilities) / REJECT, then types one line on WHY. The verification engine grades the decision AND the reasoning against a ground-truth record for that scenario, returns receipts (this dependency does not exist on PyPI; this manifest's declared scope contradicts its network calls), and adapts the next round's difficulty. Streak/lives mechanic surfaces the rubber-stamp reflex the research calls 'Lies-in-the-Loop.' A short pre-test and identical post-test bracket the run to show a measured delta.
concepts.gpt56_as_engine: GPT-5.6 is the whole product, not garnish. It runs three distinct roles: (1) GENERATOR — synthesizes non-Googleable scenario variants off a curated template spine so no two runs match and no writeup exists; (2) ADVERSARY — adapts the next action to exploit the player's demonstrated blind spot (if they rubber-stamp under time pressure, it smuggles a malicious action among benign ones); (3) CRITIC/GRADER — a separate structured pass that scores the free-form 'why' against the scenario's ground-truth facts and emits the receipts. The generator/critic split is the verification loop judges reward: the model checks its own work before the player ever sees a verdict.
concepts.verification_dna: Every round is literally grade-against-ground-truth. Scenarios are generated as variations on a verified spine (OWASP LLM Top 10, real CVE patterns like CVE-2025-53773, slopsquatting package data), and a critic pass cross-checks both the generated scenario for correctness (kills the hallucinated-lesson risk) and the player's answer against that spine, returning citable receipts rather than a flag-string match. This is Rippletide-style decision-layer verification wearing a game skin.
concepts.why_it_wins: Technological Implementation (Sottiaux, Korevec): generator+critic+adversary is a self-critiquing agent loop — Korevec's 'the crit is the product' and Sottiaux's verification obsession, made playable; a static CTF structurally cannot claim this. Design (Sottiaux, Seshan): one deep, polished, browser-runnable loop with real receipts is the 'complete coherent experience' and 'polished artifact at the end' both prize. Potential Impact (Belsky, Seshan): OWASP #1 threat in 2026, real $ losses, and it trains the exact verification reflex Belsky says vibe coders lack ('requires technical verification knowledge'); shows a named developer's pre/post score jump. Quality of Idea (Steinberger, Korevec): the empty defender lane, framed as the anti-Gandalf ('Gandalf teaches you to attack; this teaches you not to get owned'), and it teaches Codex's own sandbox+approval two-dials model back to its makers — Steinberger lived the OpenClaw crisis this dramatizes.
concepts.track: Developer Tools (primary) with an explicit Education framing in the pitch. Reasoning: the audience is developers using coding agents, the subject is Codex's own safety model, and Dev Tools puts it in front of the ex-Codex engineers (Sottiaux) and the Codex PM (Korevec) where the topicality and self-critique story land hardest. It can be cross-entered/pitched to Education (Belsky) via the pre/post measured-outcome and verification-knowledge angle, but Dev Tools is the sharper home because the toy-risk is lowest where the judges recognize the workflow as their own.
concepts.biggest_risk: Correctness of generated scenarios — a game that teaches a hallucinated exploit or wrong fix is a net negative and a security-literate judge will puncture it instantly. Mitigation: never free-invent vulns; generate only bounded variations on a hand-verified template spine, run the critic pass as a mandatory correctness gate before display, and keep a curated fallback bank so the live demo never risks a bad generation. Secondary risk: 'AI security game' reads saturated — mitigate by stating the precise novelty (defender workflow + Codex two-dials + grade-the-reasoning verification) rather than claiming to be first.

concepts.name: Skill Check
concepts.pitch: A trust-decision simulation set in a fictional-but-obvious agent-skill marketplace where you audit a skill's manifest against what it actually does, and the engine grades your verdict against the ground-truth behavior — the OpenClaw crisis turned into a workbench you can practice on.
concepts.gameplay_loop: GPT-5.6 generates a candidate 'skill' / MCP server for a marketplace: a plausible README, a declared permission manifest, and the actual source + simulated runtime network calls. The player inspects (manifest vs source vs outbound traffic), then rules INSTALL / SANDBOX / QUARANTINE and writes the tell that gave it away. The engine reveals the ground-truth (benign, or a planted stealer echoing the 341+ malicious ClawHub skills / AMOS pattern), scores the catch AND the reasoning, and escalates: later skills hide the payload better, split it across files, or spoof a trusted publisher. Progression walks the Unit 42 recommended workflow (verify provenance, line-by-line source audit, watch egress) as levels.
concepts.gpt56_as_engine: GPT-5.6 generates the full artifact triad (README + manifest + source + traffic log) with a controlled malice flag, then a critic pass verifies the artifact is internally consistent with its ground-truth label before the player sees it, and grades the player's audit reasoning against the planted tells. The adversary role escalates evasion based on what the player has been missing. Generation is what makes every skill unique and un-memorizable.
concepts.verification_dna: The core loop is provenance verification, and the grading is answer-vs-ground-truth on both the verdict and the stated tell. It directly dramatizes 'verify the agent's supply chain before you trust it' — the panel's verification center of gravity applied to the supply-chain surface Steinberger personally firefought.
concepts.why_it_wins: Technological Implementation (Korevec, Sottiaux): multi-artifact generation + a consistency-verifier pass is a heavy, legible Codex/GPT-5.6 workload. Quality of Idea (Steinberger): this is HIS crisis — 341 malicious ClawHub skills, RCE CVE, ~$2.3M stolen — rendered as practice, which is the highest-resonance idea on the board for him specifically. Potential Impact (Belsky, Seshan): supply-chain trust is a live, quantified harm and the skill maps to a real hygiene reflex. Design (Sottiaux): the audit workbench is a coherent, polished single experience. Weaker than Blast Radius on Design breadth because the loop is more analytical/slower-paced and harder to make feel delightful in a 3-min demo.
concepts.track: Developer Tools. Reasoning: it is squarely about agent/MCP supply-chain hygiene for developers and lands hardest with Steinberger and the Codex team; Education framing is available but secondary.
concepts.biggest_risk: The loop can feel like slow forensic reading rather than a game, dulling the 'delight' Sottiaux rewards and risking a flat demo. Mitigation: tight timer, visible escalation of adversary cunning, and a punchy reveal animation on each catch; keep artifacts short and skimmable. Legal/trademark risk from the ClawHub echo — mitigate with a clearly fictional marketplace name.

concepts.name: First Responder
concepts.pitch: A blue-team incident simulation where GPT-5.6 spins up a fresh compromise from real forensic artifacts and grades your triage reasoning against ground truth — putting a working investigator's judgment in the hands of anyone, backed by the contestant's 300-tool forensic engine.
concepts.gameplay_loop: GPT-5.6 generates a fresh incident scenario (suspicious process tree, event-log anomaly, a malicious persistence entry, an odd network connection) surfaced through realistic artifact snippets. The player triages: classify the finding, name the likely technique (MITRE), decide the containment move, and justify it. The engine grades the reasoning against a ground-truth incident record and returns receipts (why this parent-child chain is anomalous, which artifact corroborates it), escalating toward multi-artifact correlation. Optional depth: back the ground-truth with real outputs from his MaRa SIFT forensic MCP server so scenarios are anchored in genuine tool data, not vibes.
concepts.gpt56_as_engine: GPT-5.6 generates the incident narrative + artifact set, then a critic pass validates the scenario against the forensic ground-truth (optionally real MCP tool output) before display, and grades the player's triage chain. The adversary can role-play an intruder covering tracks (timestomp, log-clear) that the player must catch. His forensic MCP server becomes the ground-truth oracle — a genuine, unfakeable moat.
concepts.verification_dna: Grading triage reasoning against a forensic ground-truth record IS verification, and wiring MaRa SIFT in as the oracle is the strongest possible 'grade against real ground truth' claim on the board.
concepts.why_it_wins: Quality of Idea + Technological Implementation: the deepest expertise moat and a real MCP integration (heavy Codex/tooling story). Potential Impact (Belsky): 'democratizes expert investigator capability — a named analyst, a measured skill gain' hits her access/superpowers doctrine and Seshan's 'give semi-technical people superpowers.' BUT it is the weakest fit for THIS hackathon's center of gravity: classic DFIR blue-team is off the maximally-topical 'safe AI coding' message, the space (BTL1, OpenSOC, HTB) is more crowded, and standing up believable forensic artifacts is the heaviest 7-day scope. Ranked third because topicality and scope-safety both favor the AI-coding lane.
concepts.track: Education (primary), Developer Tools (secondary). Reasoning: framed as democratizing an expert investigator's judgment with a measured outcome, it is Belsky's lane; but it forfeits the Codex-team topicality that Blast Radius captures.
concepts.biggest_risk: Scope and realism — believable forensic artifacts + a genuine MCP wiring is the most build-heavy option for a solo 9-day window with Codex access only set up today, and thin artifacts read as fake to an expert. Mitigation: lean entirely on the existing MaRa SIFT server for real artifacts, ship one incident type deeply, and treat the MCP oracle as the demo centerpiece rather than breadth of scenarios.

design.final_recommendation: Build BLAST RADIUS and enter it in Developer Tools, pitched with an explicit Education/verification-knowledge frame. It is the only concept that simultaneously sits in the empty defender lane, is maximally topical to the entire Codex judging panel (it teaches Codex's own sandbox+approval two-dials model and dramatizes the real Codex CVEs), and — decisively — makes the game engine itself a verification-layer system (generator + self-critic + ground-truth grader), which converts the toy-risk into the panel's single favorite artifact class. Against the runner-up (Skill Check): both are strong and share the same engine, but Blast Radius wins on Design and demo delight because APPROVE/SANDBOX/REJECT under a fatigue timer is a fast, legible, visceral loop that reads instantly on video, whereas Skill Check's manifest-vs-source audit is slower forensic reading that risks a flat 3-minute demo; Blast Radius also covers a broader slice of the OWASP LLM/MCP threat surface (commands, deps, manifests, diffs, poisoned context) so a single deep build still feels complete. Skill Check's one edge is peak resonance with Steinberger's personal OpenClaw scar — so fold a ClawHub-style malicious-skill scenario in as ONE of Blast Radius's round types to capture that resonance without paying its pacing cost. Keep First Responder in the back pocket only if Codex/API setup slips badly and he needs to fall back to a pure-moat play; it forfeits the Codex-team topicality that is Blast Radius's biggest asset.
design.predicted_reception: Sottiaux (verification/anti-scaffolding/delight): STRONG. The generator-then-critic-then-grade loop is exactly the agent-that-verifies-its-own-output he rewarded in the Rippletide-winning hackathon; the fast approve/sandbox/reject loop with real receipts reads as delight-with-substance, not scaffolding. Risk he'll probe: is the grading real or hand-waved — mitigated by showing the critic pass and citable receipts live. Korevec (the crit is the product, dogfooding, docs): STRONG. The critic pass literally is the product; if the contestant narrates building it WITH Codex under safe-agent practices and ships a crisp AGENTS.md, he hits Korevec's dogfooding sweet spot dead center. Steinberger (lived OpenClaw, hates slideware/compliance vocab, loves live demo + heavy Codex leverage + 'my mum can use it'): STRONG-to-LOVE, conditional on execution — he will love the anti-Gandalf defender framing and the ClawHub round, but he'll walk if it smells like compliance theater or the demo isn't a fast live working loop; keep the vocabulary receipts/prove/blast-radius, never 'awareness/compliance.' Belsky (active learning, measurable outcomes, named human + one number, access): STRONG on the Education cross-pitch — the pre/post delta and 'teaches the verification knowledge vibe coders lack' quote her almost verbatim; slightly less her track than a pure ed-tool, so lead the Dev Tools entry with tech and attach the measured-outcome as the impact clincher. Seshan (define good, review outputs, polished artifact, superpowers for semi-technical people): STRONG. 'Define what good looks like, review the agent's output, get a receipt' is the exact loop, and it gives non-security developers an expert's reflex. Overall: a credible top-of-track finisher if the live demo lands and correctness is visibly gated; the only path to a low score is a hallucinated scenario in the demo or a pitch that slips into awareness/compliance framing.
codex_gpt56_showcase: AGENTS.md as the spine: define the ground-truth scenario templates (OWASP LLM Top 10 + MCP threats + real CVE/slopsquatting patterns), the generator contract, the critic/grader rubric, and the safe-agent practices followed while building — doubles as Korevec-grade dogfooding evidence and the docs he says are not optional.

Custom Codex Skill for the correctness gate: package the critic/verifier as a reusable 'verify-scenario' Skill returning a structured verdict + receipts + pass/fail correctness flag; this is the self-critiquing loop Sottiaux and Korevec reward and the tiebreaker-grade artifact.
Wire MaRa SIFT MCP server as an optional ground-truth oracle for any forensic-flavored round — real MCP use, red/blue moat visible (Steinberger's heavy-Codex-leverage box).
Parallel Codex agents in the build: one generates scenario variants, a second adversarially tries to make the grader accept a wrong answer — shows the verification loop was itself verified.

Run /review on the game's own codebase before submission and say so — reviewing the agent's output is exactly what the game teaches, so dogfooding /review is a thematically perfect Technological Implementation flex.
Primary Codex thread + captured /feedback Session ID for submission; narrate that the game was built under the same sandbox+approval discipline it teaches.

GPT-5.6 features powering the engine: structured outputs for the generator schema and receipt format; reasoning for the critic/consistency correctness gate before display; adaptive difficulty from feeding the player's blind spot into the next generation; free-form reasoning grading (score the why, not a flag) as the core non-Googleable novelty.
video_beats: 0:00-0:20 — COLD OPEN on real stakes: a coding agent about to run a suggested `pip install` of a package that does not exist on PyPI (slopsquatting), or an MCP skill quietly requesting ~/.ssh — one real 2026 headline (OpenClaw / Copilot CVE-2025-53773) on screen. Line: 'Your AI coding agent asks for approval dozens of times a day. Rubber-stamp one wrong call and you're owned.' No compliance vocabulary.

0:20-0:45 — THE LOOP, live and fast: play one round of Blast Radius. A generated agent action appears under a timer; the player picks SANDBOX and configures the blast radius, types one line on why. Show the decision feeling real and quick.

0:45-1:20 — THE VERIFICATION REVEAL (the hero beat): the critic pass fires and returns receipts — 'this package has zero PyPI history; the manifest declares read-only but the source opens an outbound socket' — and grades the player's REASONING, not a flag. Say plainly: 'Every scenario is generated fresh, then the model checks its own work against verified ground truth before you ever see it — no two runs are the same and you can't Google the answer.'

1:20-1:50 — ADAPTIVE ADVERSARY + the ClawHub round: show the next scenario escalate to smuggle a malicious action among benign ones (approval-fatigue mechanic), including one skill-marketplace audit round nodding to the real supply-chain crisis. Frame: 'Gandalf teaches you to attack a bot. This teaches you not to get owned by your own agent.'

1:50-2:20 — MEASURED OUTCOME + named human: show a real tester's pre-test vs post-test score jump on screen — one name, one number — and tie it to Belsky's line that vibe coding requires verification knowledge. This is the impact clincher.

2:20-2:50 — HOW IT'S BUILT (Technological Implementation, required in video): 30 seconds on Codex — AGENTS.md spine, the custom verify-scenario Skill running the critic gate, GPT-5.6 as generator+adversary+grader, and 'built by a Codex agent under the same sandbox+approval discipline this game teaches.' Flash the /feedback Session ID.
2:50-3:00 — CLOSE on the one-liner: 'Blast Radius. Learn to review your agent before it reviews your paycheck.' Show the browser URL — free, no install.
design.biggest_unknown: Whether GPT-5.6 can reliably GENERATE security scenarios that are correct AND make its own critic pass catch the rare bad generation before the player sees it — i.e. whether the self-verification loop is trustworthy enough that a security-literate judge never sees a hallucinated exploit or a mis-grade in the live demo. This is the make-or-break: it is both the project's core novelty and its core failure mode. It must be de-risked in the first 2 days by building the template spine + critic gate first and keeping a curated fallback scenario bank so the demo cannot be tanked by a live bad generation.

Must build with Codex + GPT-5.6. Tracks: Apps for Your Life, Work & Productivity, Developer Tools, Education. NEW DIRECTION he wants to explore: an EDU…
